title: PHY.4-1A — Introduction to Waves
description: Learn about Introduction to Waves in GCSE Science Physics
learningOutcomes: Recall that waves transfer energy and information without transferring matter.
Describe the behavior of particles in a medium as a wave passes through.
Identify evidence from water or sound waves showing that the medium remains in place.

outcomes.text: Recall that waves transfer energy and information without transferring matter.
Describe the behavior of particles in a medium as a wave passes through.
Identify evidence from water or sound waves showing that the medium remains in place.

terms.definition: A wave is a periodic disturbance that transfers energy and information from one point to another without the permanent displacement of matter.
A medium is the physical material or substance through which a wave travels, which remains in its original location after the energy has passed.
Oscillation is the repetitive back-and-forth or up-and-down motion of particles about a fixed position, not to be confused with the overall travel of the wave itself.
Energy transfer is the process by which a wave moves power or data through a system, differing from matter transfer because the particles of the medium do not travel with the wave.
Displacement is the distance and direction an individual particle moves from its original resting position as a wave passes through it.

content.title: Introduction to Waves Diagram
content.description: Visual representation of Introduction to Waves
content.placeholderText: The diagram should show a side-view cross-section of a water wave. A 'resting-position' line should run horizontally through the center. A 'bobbing-duck' is placed on the 'water-surface', which is curved into a wave shape. An arrow labeled 'wave-direction' points to the right. Small circular arrows or vertical double-headed arrows labeled 'particle-oscillation' should be shown under the duck to demonstrate that the water moves up and down, while the duck stays at the same horizontal coordinate. This illustrates that the wave moves right, but the medium stays in place.

content.title: Energy and Information Transfer
content.content:
### In this lesson
In this lesson, you will learn how waves act as messengers that carry energy and data from one place to another. This is the foundation for understanding everything from how we hear music to how the internet works through fiber-optic cables.

*   Waves move energy, not the material they travel through.
*   Information can be carried by a wave without moving matter.
*   Particles in a wave move around a fixed point.

**What this is**
A **Wave** is a periodic disturbance that transfers energy and information from one point to another without the permanent displacement of matter. Imagine you are holding one end of a long slinky and your friend is holding the other. If you give it a quick wiggle, a bump travels all the way to your friend. That bump is the wave! Even though the bump reached your friend, the metal rings of the slinky stayed in your hands or near your friend. The material the wave travels through is called the **Medium**. In our slinky example, the slinky itself is the **Medium**.

**Why it matters**
Understanding how waves work is like learning the secret language of the universe. It explains how a radio station in another city can send music to your car without throwing physical CDs through the air! By using **Energy Transfer**, we can send signals across the ocean or even from satellites in space. It allows us to communicate and move power efficiently without having to transport heavy physical objects every time we want to send a message.

**Key facts / rules**
*   Waves carry energy from a source to a receiver.
*   Waves can carry information, such as sounds or digital signals.
*   The **Medium** does not travel along with the wave.
*   Particles in the **Medium** undergo **Oscillation**, moving back and forth or up and down.
*   After the wave passes, the **Medium** returns to its original position.
*   **Displacement** measures how far a particle has moved from its resting spot.

**How to recognise it**
You can recognise a wave by looking for a pattern that moves across a distance while the parts making it up stay in the same general area. Think of a "Mexican Wave" at a sports stadium. You see a giant ripple move all the way around the seats. However, the people (the **Medium**) aren't running around the track! They simply stand up and sit down—this is their **Oscillation**. Because the people stay in their seats, you know it is a wave and not a group of people running in a circle.

**Common mistakes**
Learners often confuse **Energy Transfer** with matter transfer; **Energy Transfer** is the movement of the wave's signal across a distance, whereas matter transfer would mean the physical particles move from the start to the end. 

Misconceptions (targeted):
*   Misconception: When you talk, the air from your mouth travels to the listener’s ear. | Correction: The air particles only vibrate back and forth, passing the energy along, but the air stays near you. | AC: AC4.1
*   Misconception: Waves are physical objects like "water bullets." | Correction: A wave is a pattern of movement traveling through a substance, not a solid object moving through space. | AC: AC4.1

**Key Points**
*   Waves transfer energy and information.
*   The **Medium** stays in its original location.
*   **Oscillation** is the repetitive motion of the particles.
*   **Displacement** is the temporary move from the resting position.

**Quick recap**
Waves are amazing because they allow us to move energy and information over huge distances without actually moving any "stuff" permanently. Whether it's a ripple in a pond or a signal on your phone, the particles in the **Medium** just wiggle (oscillate) and then go back to where they started. This process of **Energy Transfer** is what makes our modern world work!

### Coming Up Next
Next, we will look at the specific evidence we can see in the real world that proves the water and air don't actually go anywhere when a wave passes through them!

content.title: Check Your Understanding: Energy and Information Transfer

questions.questionText: What is transferred by a wave instead of the material it travels through?
questions.hint: Think about what the wave is 'carrying' to the receiver.

questions.questionText: Where do particles in a wave move in relation to their starting position?
questions.hint: The particles wiggle but do not leave their general area.

questions.questionText: What does not travel along with the wave as it moves from one place to another?
questions.hint: Identify the term for the substance the wave travels through.

questions.questionText: Using your answers to Q1 (energy transfer), Q2 (particle movement), and Q3 (the medium), explain how these facts connect. Why does this relationship matter?
keyPoints: Energy moves across a distance while the medium stays in place
Particles only oscillate around fixed points to pass the signal
This allows communication without moving heavy physical objects
Information transfer is possible because only the disturbance travels
expectedAnswer: Waves are efficient because they move energy and information across a distance while the medium stays in place. Because particles only move around a fixed point, we can send signals without having to physically transport matter.

This relationship matters because it explains how information travels without matter transfer. The energy passes through the medium, but the medium itself does not travel along, meaning only the wiggling particles are needed to move the signal.
questions.hint: Think about why we don't need to throw physical objects to send a message.

content.title: Evidence of Wave Motion
content.content:
### In this lesson
You will explore the observable evidence that proves waves travel through a substance without taking the substance with them. We will look at water ripples and sound waves to see physics in action.

*   Water waves provide visual proof that the medium stays put.
*   Sound waves move through air without creating a wind.
*   Particle behavior is defined by temporary **Displacement**.

**What this is**
When we talk about waves, we need to prove that the **Medium** remains in place. This is called "evidence of wave motion." For example, when a sound travels through the air, it is an **Energy Transfer** where air particles push against each other in a repetitive **Oscillation**. The particles don't actually fly across the room. If they did, every time someone spoke to you, you would feel a strong gust of wind hitting your face! Since you don't feel a breeze when someone talks, we know the air is staying put.

**Why it matters**
Knowing that the **Medium** stays in place helps scientists design better technology. If water waves actually moved the water across the ocean, the sea levels would pile up on one side of the world! By understanding that the particles only experience a temporary **Displacement**, we can predict how waves will behave when they hit obstacles or move from the deep ocean to the beach.

**Key facts / rules**
*   A floating object on water moves up and down but not sideways with the wave.
*   Sound waves are vibrations that pass through air particles.
*   **Displacement** is temporary; particles return to "equilibrium" (rest).
*   Waves move through the **Medium**, they do not take it with them.
*   In water waves, the "humps" move, but the water molecules mostly move in circles or up and down.
*   In sound waves, air molecules move back and forth in the direction of the wave.

**How to recognise it**
You can recognise this behavior by observing a "marker" in the **Medium**. Imagine a rubber duck floating on a calm pond. If you drop a stone nearby, ripples will move outward. Typically, you will see the duck bob up and down or rock back and forth as the wave passes. Crucially, the duck does not get pushed to the edge of the pond by the ripples. The duck’s motion shows us exactly what the water particles are doing: they are undergoing **Oscillation** but staying in the same spot. 

Micro-scenario: If a wave with a height of 20cm passes under a duck, the duck's maximum **Displacement** is 20cm from its resting position. However, after the wave has traveled 10 meters away, the duck is still in its original starting spot.

**Common mistakes**
Learners often confuse **Oscillation** with the overall travel of the wave; **Oscillation** is the local back-and-forth movement, whereas the wave travel is the movement of the energy pulse itself. 

Misconceptions (targeted):
*   Misconception: Waves in the ocean move the water from the middle of the sea to the beach. | Correction: The energy moves to the beach, but the water particles mostly bob in place until they "break" at the shore. | AC: AC4.2
*   Misconception: If a sound is louder, the air particles must be traveling further across the room. | Correction: A louder sound means the particles have a larger **Displacement** (they wiggle harder), but they still stay in the same area. | AC: AC4.2

**Key Points**
*   Floating objects provide evidence that water doesn't travel with the wave.
*   The lack of "wind" from speakers proves air particles stay in place.
*   Particles return to their resting position after the wave passes.
*   Waves move energy, not matter.

**Quick recap**
We can see and hear evidence every day that waves don't move matter. Whether it's a duck bobbing on a pond or the fact that your hair doesn't blow around when you listen to music, the evidence is clear: the **Medium** stays, and the energy travels. The particles just move a little bit (their **Displacement**) and then settle back down once the **Wave** has passed by.

### Coming Up Next
Now that we know what waves are, we'll learn how to measure them using terms like frequency and wavelength!

content.title: Check Your Understanding: Evidence of Wave Motion

questions.questionText: How does a floating object move when a wave passes it on the water?
expectedAnswer: Up and down but not sideways
questions.hint: Consider the vertical motion of a marker like a rubber duck.

questions.questionText: What observable effect is absent when sound waves travel through the air?
questions.hint: Think about what you would feel on your face if the air particles actually moved across the room.

questions.questionText: What is the relationship between a wave and the medium it travels through?
expectedAnswer: Do not take it with them
questions.hint: Does the wave carry the substance along with it or just pass through it?

questions.questionText: Using your answers to Q1 (floating object motion), Q2 (lack of wind), and Q3 (the medium), explain how these facts connect. How do we know this connection is valid?
keyPoints: Floating objects and the lack of wind are visual and physical evidence
These observations prove that waves move through the medium without taking it with them
The up and down motion shows particles stay in one area
If the medium moved, we would see sideways drift and feel air currents
expectedAnswer: We know waves don't take the medium with them because a floating object only moves up and down and we don't feel a wind when sound travels. These observations prove that the energy moves through the substance while the particles stay in their general area.

The connection is valid because if waves did move the medium, we would feel a breeze from speakers and see ducks pushed to the edge of a pond. Since the duck bobs and there is no wind, we know the wave travels through the medium without taking it along.
questions.hint: Connect the lack of physical 'pushing' to the idea that the medium stays put.

questions.questionText: How do we know that sound waves traveling through the air do not physically move the air mass from the speaker to the listener?
questions.hint: Think about what you would feel on your face if the air particles were actually traveling across the room.

questions.questionText: A wave passes through a medium, causing a particle to move 14mm away from its resting position. What is the numeric value of the displacement at this moment?
questions.hint: Displacement is the distance a particle moves from its original resting spot. Provide the answer in mm.

questions.questionText: In a fiber-optic system, digital information is sent as light waves through a glass cable. What must be true about the glass particles (the medium) once the information has reached its destination?
questions.hint: Consider the rule regarding the permanent displacement of matter when a wave passes through a medium.

questions.questionText: A marker is floating on a liquid at a height of 5 meters. A series of waves passes through the liquid, causing the marker to oscillate. What is the height of the marker after all the waves have passed and the liquid is calm again?
questions.hint: Recall what happens to the medium's position after the energy of a wave has finished moving through it. Provide the answer in meters.

questions.questionText: What is the technical term for the repetitive back-and-forth or up-and-down motion that particles perform as a wave passes them?
questions.hint: This term describes the movement around a fixed point, not the travel of the wave itself.

questions.questionText: Explain the relationship between particle displacement and the transfer of information across a medium. In your answer, include: (1) the movement of particles relative to their fixed positions, (2) the distinction between matter transport and energy transfer, and (3) how the pattern of disturbance carries data. Why does the lack of net matter movement confirm that energy is the primary output of this process?
keyPoints: Identifies that particles oscillate or move around a fixed reference point.
Explains that energy is passed between particles while the particles themselves return to their origin.
Describes how the sequence or pattern of the disturbance encodes information.
Reasons that if matter were transported, the system would be a flow or current rather than a wave.
Concludes that the medium acts only as a carrier for the energy state.
expectedAnswer: Particles in a medium move temporarily around a fixed point, passing energy to their neighbors without moving along with the wave. This allows information to be transmitted as a pattern of disturbances rather than a physical delivery of matter. We know energy is the primary output because the medium remains in its original location after the wave passes, proving that only the state of motion was transferred.

In a wave, particles undergo temporary displacement but do not experience net migration, which distinguishes energy transfer from matter transport. Information is carried through the specific frequency or timing of these particle movements. This relationship holds because if the medium moved with the wave, it would require a continuous supply of new matter rather than a self-sustaining periodic disturbance.
questions.hint: Focus on what happens to an individual particle after the wave has passed and what that implies about the transport of energy.

questions.questionText: Synthesize the principles of wave motion to explain how a signal can be transmitted across a distance without shifting the physical environment. In your answer, include: (1) the role of energy and information transfer, (2) the specific behavior of the medium’s particles, (3) observable evidence from water or sound that the medium remains in place, and (4) the necessity of temporary displacement in this system. Answer in 3-4 sentences OR concise bullet points.
keyPoints: Waves serve as the mechanism for moving energy and data across distances.
The medium provides the physical substrate for the wave but does not travel with it.
Particles experience temporary displacement from an equilibrium position.
Evidence includes objects bobbing on water without moving forward with the ripples.
Evidence includes sound traveling through air without creating a constant wind or air current.
Temporary displacement is required to allow the disturbance to propagate continuously.
The stability of the medium confirms that waves are a transfer of state, not substance.
Information is preserved through the consistency of the particle oscillations.
expectedAnswer: Waves transmit energy and information by causing particles in a medium to undergo temporary displacement around a fixed point. Evidence for this is seen in water ripples where a floating object bobs in place rather than traveling with the wave, or sound moving through air without creating wind. This system requires that particles return to their original positions so that the medium remains stable while the signal passes through it.

• Waves act as messengers that carry energy and data through a substance without transporting the substance itself.
• Particles within the medium move temporarily away from their equilibrium position before returning, facilitating the transfer of energy to adjacent particles.
• We observe this when sound travels through a room without moving the air mass, or when water waves pass under a stationary buoy.
• This temporary displacement is essential because it allows the wave to move across the environment while the physical medium stays in place.
questions.hint: Combine the concept of particle oscillation with the real-world observations of water and sound to explain the efficiency of wave transmission.

content.title: Foundation Check

questions.questionText: What is the general term for any physical substance that has mass and occupies space?
questions.hint: It is the material that all physical objects are made of.

questions.questionText: What term is used to describe the tiny individual units that make up a material or substance?
questions.hint: Think about the smallest building blocks of a medium.

questions.questionText: What is the term for a material or substance, such as water or air, through which an effect can travel?
questions.hint: This word describes the environment or carrier for the wave.

content.notes: Foundation check before starting Introduction to Waves

scope.inScope: The conceptual understanding of waves as energy transfers and the distinction between wave travel and medium displacement.
scope.outOfScope: Mathematical definitions of frequency/wavelength, wave equations, and classification of transverse or longitudinal waves.
scope.rationale: This introductory lesson establishes the physical nature of waves before layering on technical vocabulary and calculations found in later ACs.

teachingConstraints.specificScope: Recall of energy transfer and identification of evidence regarding medium movement only.

misconceptions.misconception: When you talk, the air from your mouth travels all the way to the listener’s ear.
misconceptions.correction: The air particles only vibrate back and forth (oscillate), passing the energy along like a relay race, but the air itself stays near you.

misconceptions.misconception: Waves in the ocean move the water molecules from the middle of the sea all the way to the beach.
misconceptions.correction: The energy moves to the beach, but the water particles mostly bob up and down in place; only the 'shape' of the wave travels across the surface.
